data.title: WAGN: A Zero S&P-500 Overlap ETF
data.description:
Pabrai Wagons ETF (WAGN) was effectively launched in February 2026. The current ETF- WAGN is the successor of the previous Pabrai Wagons fund which was set up as a mutual fund. The predecessor fund was launched in September 2023. Leaving that distinction aside, the current ETF is not a fresh strategy but just a wrapper changes from the previous mutual fund to garner more liquidity for investors.

The particular facts and the scope of analysis for the current fund, however, differ significantly from other ETFs. For starters, this is not a typical fund focusing on institutional imperatives or trying to fit itself into buckets, whether sector, themes, style, factors or anything else for that matter.

The manager of the fund, Mohnish Pabrai, is typically known to be a value-oriented investment manager in a contextual manner, since he does not distinguish between value and growth in popular sense. The reason of mentioning the fund manager has again to do with the nature of WAGN. Mr. Pabrai happens to be the sole investment manager here and eventually this fund could be referred to as an extraction of his investment framework on a retail-centric ETF scale. The fund is a pure bottom-up security selection approach and usually prefers — not due to mandate, but due to opportunity — to invest in companies that Mr. Market offers when they are out of favor, rather than those everyone is talking about. That is done to offer asymmetric payoffs if the thesis is correct, and downside to be significantly controlled.

Mr. Pabrai is also known for his book The Dhandho Investor and an analogy “Heads I win; Tails I don’t lose much” — and seems to follow it to a significant extent in his investment decisions. Particularly, while investing in this fund, having confidence in the style and thought process of the manager is as important as evaluating portfolio composition and performance track records.

Performance Evaluation

Even though the style and the though process of the investment manager is solid, the fund states underperformance from S&P 500 since inception. At the same time, however, since the fund commenced operations only recently in September 2023, this is not an adequate time frame to judge performance.

In addition, the recent outperformance over the last one-year period may be suggestive of some degree of mean reversion in the underlying securities positions of the fund. Although this is not conclusive, but on value-oriented (in a definitional sense) funds, a chance of superior performance cannot be excluded following a period of underperformance.

Still, the mutual fund, now converted to an ETF vehicle. WAGN is yet to meet the test of a true long-term investment track record to allow it into the portfolios of more prudent investors who prefer to wait until performance history is more established.

Portfolio Composition at Present

This is an important aspect for analyzing WAGN. Not narrations, but what the ETF actually owns would be a correct reflection of the fund's investment framework and of the investors for whom that style makes sense.

The current top ten holdings make up ~66% of the portfolio. The total number of holdings is around ~24. Kindly note that this is an active fund, and any position could be modified, added, or divested at any time. Therefore, any reliance on current positions as a proxy to evaluate the fund’s expected returns would be improper, as would attempting to replicate the allocations.

The above portfolio composition neatly reflects the classical Pabrai ideology of looking for asymmetric payoff ideas. Contrary to what many might believe, this is not a replication of modern-day Warren Buffett-like investing, nor is it an attempt to buy compounders, as seen in funds like Nalanda Capital. These are high-variance asymmetric bets.

Warrior Met Coal, Inc. (HCC) is ~11% of portfolio and Alpha Metallurgical Resources, Inc. (AMR) ~5% of portfolio — Both are metallurgical coal producers trading at depressed valuation multiples relative to their asset values and through-cycle earning power, with coal prices also in a weak phase of the cycle. The asymmetric setup here is dual-layered: if coal prices increase, due to higher operating leverage, both companies stand to benefit from improved operational performance as well as a possible re-rating of the earnings multiple.

Similarly, Edelweiss Financial Services, an Indian financial firm, recently undertook value unlocking by selling a ~4% stake in its subsidiary EAAA Alternatives. While I do not comment on the impact of these developments in isolation, what this reflects is the tendency of the fund not to buy growth at a reasonable price, but rather to focus on ideas with identifiable catalysts, whether in the form of a sector being out of favor, cheap multiples, low expectations, or some corporate actions being underway.

This assertion is again supported by other holdings Transocean Ltd. (RIG) and Valaris Limited (VAL) again recently announced the merger. Offshore drilling contractors operating in an asset-heavy, capital-intensive cyclical industry, again reflecting the fund manager’s preference for out-of-favor, cyclically depressed situations with favorable risk-reward prospects rather than premium-valuation consumer franchises or asset-light models.

Kindly note I have commented on a few individual holdings merely to draw out the point of the investment decisions made by the fund and this is neither reflective of any views, whether positive or negative, on any security. The scope of this analysis is to shed light on WAGN and not on a sector or a style.

The discussion on portfolio would also, not be complete without highlighting a subtle but key aspects of the investment mandate for this ETF: WAGN can invest up to ~25% in a single issuer. This not only resembles my personal views on the subject of portfolio allocation but is also a key aspect of bottom-up, business-oriented security selection. Here one is exploiting the active alpha in individual security returns and is not really trying to invest in equity-markets as an asset class. Although the fund mentions a limit of no more than 25%, I would take it as a positive that when the fund sees an opportunity, it has enough mandate to sufficiently allocate capital to that idea to generate meaningful alpha. This is further enhanced by the fund generally not trimming a position just because it has risen significantly or exceeded some vague allocation thresholds.

In my opinion taking all this account, the WAGN at present is suitable for allocating capital but only for investors who agree with the investment framework of the fund managers. This would therefore majorly be liked by value-oriented investors who desire a fund manager to select specific security for them. This is not a beta or a factor or a sector fund which sets itself in asset allocation of an investor but rather a partnership vehicle to a shared exposure in investment ideas with favorable risk reward prospects.

The expense ratio of WAGN is 0.9%. This may come as a surprise to many, but in my view, this is still low for a fund with an active investment mandate executing somewhat concentrated investment ideas with deep security-specific research.

Latest Investors Calls and Framing Forward Expectations

WAGN held its latest shareholders call on March 11, 2026. Like I mentioned at the outset, the fund does not like to fit itself into definitional buckets. Even where I have described the nature of the fund as value-oriented, I have emphasized that the term is used in a context-specific manner.

This was well articulated by Mr. Pabrai at the beginning of the call:

In general, stocks and businesses do not fall neatly into different buckets. As Warren Buffett has said, value and growth are not mutually exclusive. In fact, all intelligent investing is value investing. The notion that a cyclical—quote-unquote “cyclical”—is fundamentally different from a compounder or a cannibal, for example, is also myopic.

A number of queries during the call were security-specific and need not be commented upon here. However, one question addressed the fund's long-term expectations relative to the S&P 500, to which Mr. Pabrai responded:

If we did not think we could do better than the S&P long term, there would be no point in doing this. The ETF is well-positioned to have a good run against the S&P for quite a while. The S&P, as we sit today, is heavily concentrated with the Mag 7 and some other tech names, and underrepresented in some of the other businesses. I think the S&P will have some difficulties in the next 10 to 15 years because the run-ups have been so much and the valuations are so high. We will see. But yes, we definitely expect to beat the S&P over the long term. However, that is just an expectation. There are no guarantees.

This remark appropriate frames the expectations which an investor might have with the fund. Importantly, the fund is explicit about its benchmark, even while operating with a non-overlapping portfolio. Regardless of style, if the fund is not able to outperform a broad market index, there remains not much rationale for an investor to allocate. The observation regarding heavy concentration in mega-cap technology and elevated valuations is very-well founded. Indicators such as the market cap to GDP (Buffet Indicator) and broader valuation measures suggest a richly valued broader stock market environment. While this is not an analysis of the broader market, but just to contextualize and reflect on the points made by the Portfolio Manager.

I have attached the below graph to visualize the point. It does not matter at what are the exact levels reflected on this graphs and metrics but is sufficient to assert that at present S&P 500 is richly valued.

Further, this is how WAGN tries to differentiate itself from the broader market. The fund has zero overlap with the S&P 500 — a fact that is also actively highlighted by the ETF. The current multiple of WAGN is lower than that of the S&P, and on a normalized basis, there is a possibility that the gap could be greater, given that certain holdings of the portfolio are exposed to cyclical businesses where reported earnings may not fully reflect through-cycle earning power. A normalized earnings multiple for these businesses may reduce the apparent P/E, potentially making the valuation differential versus the S&P more pronounced than headline figures suggest.

For investors focused on the near term, this fund may not be particularly suitable. While certain holdings may benefit from restructuring or cyclical recovery, potentially leading to medium-term outperformance, the active nature of the fund makes timing-based entry or exit imprudent. The characteristics described are relevant over a long-term horizon, not for tactical positioning. This is not a factor or thematic exposure, but rather a bet on the investment framework and methodology of the manager. Therefore, it is suitable for investors willing to allocate capital with an appropriate time-horizon and not setting targets for entry and exit. Even then, the allocation might warrant an active revisit of the thesis, considering the active nature of the fund. Long-term investing here is also not passive; and may need ongoing monitoring.

Limitations to The Buy Case

While my write-up thus far largely paints a positive picture for this ETF, there are a few nuances which should not be ignored. The fund portfolio usually consists not of classic compounders but of ideas with favorable risk-reward prospects. Time might be a friend of the former type of businesses, but in the current environment, these cheap and cyclical stocks could continue to lag while the broader market takes off. This was indeed reflected in the since-inception performance track record, although recent one-year returns suggest some degree of normalization may be underway.

In addition, investors concerned about concentration, position sizing, or the investment framework used by the ETF should, by and large, stay out. The allocation is as much a bet on the underlying investment framework used by the Portfolio Manager as it is on the fund itself. The fund is effectively an extension of Mohnish Pabrai and is uniquely managed; both performance and reputation are tightly linked to the manager. Therefore, the human element cannot be separated, and investors who prefer systematic, data-driven approaches over high discretion may want to avoid or keep it on hold for now.

Further, this ETF might also not be suitable for benchmark-tracking exposure, especially for those who cannot tolerate multi-year underperformance relative to the S&P 500. This also makes WAGN inherently unpredictable in terms of estimating future expected returns. Another factor, not immediately pressing but structurally relevant, is capacity constraint — as AUM scales, the alpha potential would likely diminish over time. In addition, the active nature of the fund places limits on the analysis that can be done, and an investor would need to actively evaluate both the stance and their conviction in the fund manager while holding this ETF.

In Conclusion

I would rate WAGN as a buy. In my opinion, the investment framework of this ETF is strong both on a standalone basis, with a focus on finding propositions with an asymmetric payoff profile and on a relative basis versus the broader stock market and other factor-oriented funds that often fail to deliver across cycles. WAGN, by virtue of being a deeply bottom-up, security-specific, sector-agnostic ETF with high concentration, detaches itself from broader markets in important ways. Further, the underlying margin of safety typically possessed by the holdings limits the risk of permanent capital loss to a meaningful extent. However. an investor should be mindful if their personal asset allocation objectives also compliment with this fund, failing which it might not be prudent to allocate.

data.title: EMXC: The Case For Emerging Markets Without China
data.description:
The tariff war didn't create the ex-China EM thesis. It just made it impossible to ignore.

By the time Trump's reciprocal tariffs hit 125% on Chinese goods in mid-2025 and the new Section 301 investigations landed on 60-plus trading partners in March 2026, most EM investors had already done the math. China, at roughly 25% of the MSCI EM index, wasn't adding alpha. It was absorbing it. Property sector stress, household balance sheet paralysis, regulatory uncertainty, and a stock market that moves on policy rumors rather than fundamentals had turned what was once the crown jewel of emerging markets into a performance drag you were paying EEM's 0.72% expense ratio to own. The iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ex China ETF (EMXC) is the response to that math problem.

Strip Out the Label and See What You're Actually Buying

EMXC is a BlackRock iShares product launched in July 2017, tracking the MSCI Emerging Markets ex China Index. It carries a 0.25% expense ratio, holds 640 positions, has $18.3 billion in assets, and pays semi-annual distributions with a trailing 12-month yield of 2.33%. The basics are clean. But here's what rarely gets said plainly: this is not a diversified emerging markets fund in any traditional sense. When you remove China and look at what's left, a very specific bet reveals itself.

Where Your Money Actually Ends Up

TSMC sits at 17.79% of EMXC. Samsung comes in at 7.67%. SK Hynix at 4.13%. Those three names alone, all semiconductor companies plugged into the global AI infrastructure cycle, represent roughly 30% of the entire ETF. Foxconn, MediaTek, and Delta Electronics round out the Taiwan sleeve. HDFC Bank, Reliance Industries, and ICICI Bank provide India exposure. Geographically, Taiwan is 29.92%, South Korea is 22.20%, and India is 16.84%. Taiwan plus Korea combined is over 52%. The IT sector accounts for 41% of the index versus roughly 32% for MSCI EM with China included.

What am I saying here? EMXC is essentially a semiconductor ETF with EM diversification around the edges. I don't think that's a bad thing in 2026 given TSMC's capex cycle ($52 to $56 billion planned for 2026, up 27 to 37% year-over-year), but investors should go in with eyes open. Calling it a "diversified EM allocation" is generous.

Five Years of Data the China Bulls Don't Want You to See

So why does EMXC keep outperforming? The gap versus EEM over five years is blunt. EMXC: +8.39% annualized. EEM: +3.68%. That's a 471 basis-point annual advantage, compounding. The 5-year Sharpe ratio is 0.50 for EMXC versus 0.20 for EEM. Year-to-date 2026, EMXC is up roughly 10% against EEM's +4.39%. And the real gut punch: MCHI, the iShares China large-cap ETF, is down about 8% annualized over the same five-year window. EMXC beats it by roughly 1,667 basis points per year.

And EMXC is cheaper. At 0.25%, it saves 47 basis points annually versus EEM's 0.72% fee. The China drag on EEM is quantifiable at this point. It's not a hypothesis anymore.

The Three Things Running in EMXC's Favor Right Now

Friend-shoring, the AI semiconductor supercycle, and dollar weakness are all moving in the same direction at once.

The trade war has, paradoxically, benefited ex-China EM. India, Taiwan, Vietnam, and Mexico have emerged as logical beneficiaries of supply chain reallocation away from China. EMXC investors sidestep Chinese tariff risk entirely while capturing those realignment flows. Goldman Sachs projected roughly 15% MSCI India earnings growth in 2026, a recovery after India underperformed EM by the widest margin in three decades last year. After $18 billion in foreign selling of Indian equities in 2025, there is a genuine mean-reversion setup here. On the semiconductor side, TSMC guided for up to $34.6 to $35.8 billion in Q1 2026 revenue (approximately 38% year-over-year growth). Estimated 2026 earnings growth for MSCI EM IT has been revised from 10% in September 2025 to over 92% now. The dollar, which ran a 15-year bull cycle, appears to be rolling over. This is the rotation I've been watching in The Lead-Lag Report.

What Could Go Wrong (And Some of This Is Serious)

Taiwan at 29.92% is a concentration story, not a diversification story. If there's a Taiwan Strait escalation, TSMC, at 17.79%, gets hit hard. A 20% correction in TSMC alone would shave roughly 3.5 percentage points off EMXC's NAV before you account for sympathy declines in Foxconn, MediaTek, and Delta Electronics. A negative, in my view, if you haven't modeled that scenario.

India-Pakistan tensions have been elevated since Operation Sindoor in May 2025, and the U.S. Intelligence Community's March 2026 threat assessment flagged nuclear escalation risk explicitly. India is 16.84% of EMXC. Korea carries a North Korea overhang that markets perpetually underestimate. Then there are fees: Vanguard launched VEXC in September 2025 at 0.07%, which is 18 basis points cheaper than EMXC. That's a real competitive threat long-term, even if EMXC retains a massive liquidity advantage at roughly $300 million or more in daily dollar volume. EMXC already pulled back from its February 2026 all-time high near $88 to around $80 by early April. This isn't a low-volatility vehicle.

Bottom Line

In my view, EMXC is the most liquid and cleanest institutional expression of the ex-China EM rotation, and the five-year performance record makes the China exclusion case better than any argument I could write. But own it knowing it's a concentrated semiconductor and financials bet in geopolitically complex markets, not a low-risk diversifier.

data.title: The Market Forgot Nvidia: Big Mistake
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), once the undisputed king of AI, seems to have been forgotten by investors.

Interest has moved beyond GPUs and into the new AI bottlenecks like memory. And even within GPUs, Nvidia’s dominance is being challenged.

But does that justify the current price action in Nvidia?

The stock has been flat in the last six months, while earnings keep expanding and AI demand continues to heat up.

Nvidia is now the cheapest it has ever been in this AI cycle, if you ask me, and this sets it up as an outperformer in the coming months.

Nvidia Stock’s Stagnation

The stock has been pretty much flat for over six months now. Historically, we've seen this happen before, and it's usually been followed by a rally.

It happened back in 2024 and also in 2021. It’s understandable. Sometimes expectations get ahead of themselves, and both fundamentals and technicals have to reset.

But we’ve waited long enough.

I highlighted how Nvidia is now a critical strategic asset in my last article, and why it deserves a higher valuation.

Today, we highlight this further with some clear evidence from the GPU market.

Valuation Metrics

Let’s begin by stating the obvious. Nvidia is now historically undervalued.

The stock is trading well below its 5 YR average on most valuation metrics. Forward P/E of 21,46 is very compelling, and of course, both the PEG and forward PEG.

This makes no sense, especially when the rest of the AI market is very much chugging along.

The AI Divergence

While Nvidia has stagnated, all other areas of the AI market are lighting up.

Memory stocks, like Micron (MU) have exploded higher, and fiber optics stocks like Lumentum (LITE) have also seen a great repricing.

Even within the more strictly related semiconductor stocks, NVDA is a laggard. Both Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Broadcom (AVGO) have outperformed Nvidia.

Why The NVDA Discount Makes No Sense

You might think that these lesser-known AI stocks have just been playing catch-up in terms of valuation, but that’s a line we crossed a while ago.

Nvidia is cheaper than most of the stocks listed above, with the exception of Micron, which I am also very bullish on.

You could argue that while memory and connectivity are now bottlenecks, the market is now well supplied with GPUs.

But that’s not what price is signaling.

As we can see from this chart, GPU prices have moved higher and higher in the last 5 years, while Nvidia’s P/E has continued to fall.

While perhaps GPU prices did stagnate in 2025, we are beginning to see GPU rental prices pick up on 2026, signaling increasing demand.

The rental price for an H200, for example, has more than doubled in price since the beginning of the year.

This bodes very well for Nvidia, and points to a divergence that I cannot understand.

Price Target

Nvidia is still growing at a healthy rate, and there’s even potential for further margin expansion.

Plus, this is still the company that saw AI coming years in advance, and that should count for something.

Nvidia is projected to reach around $8.30 EPS by year-end, up over 73% from last year.

Even applying a modest P/E of 25, that would put Nvidia in the $222 price range, roughly a 23% increase from today’s price, and likely in the low range.

More likely, Nvidia will beat expectations as AI heats up, and a shift in sentiment could push the P/E closer to previous levels of around 30.

At 30 P/E and EPS closer to 9, we’re looking at a price range of closer to $270.

This lines up better with my technical analysis, in which I have used a three-point fib extension to project some key fib levels. The 1.618 gets us just shy of $300, an important psychological threshold, too.

Nvidia could arguably be overvalued at that point, but once animal spirits take over, and if the AI narrative picks up more steam, this is within reach.

The Issue With Nvidia

The problem a lot of people have with Nvidia, perhaps, is that it’s already too big.

It controls over 90% of the GPU market, and some hyperscalers are deliberately trying to diversify away from Nvidia in order to not to be locked in.

While there’s some merit to this, and it’s the reason I have been more bullish on AMD in the last year, this narrative is already priced in, and Nvidia has, in fact, done a lot to address this issue.

For example, Nvidia has released a lot of open-source models. making it easy for everyone to participate in the ecosystem.

On the other hand, yes, Nvidia is trying to sell the whole AI factory. The issue here is whether the whole Nvidia ecosystem is compelling enough for its clients to go all in on it.

If market share and performance is any indication, then Nvidia still has a commanding lead and will continue to grow in line with the AI market.

Final Thoughts

The market has rightly, in my opinion, been chasing the AI narrative. But it has left Nvidia behind.

We now have a compelling entry point, and I believe Nvidia could begin to outperform, especially as other AI names that have run too far begin to deflate, and Nvidia continues to prove to the market why it’s still the king of AI.

data.title: 3 Reasons Why I Remain Bullish On SCHB, And 2 To Sell It Immediately
data.description:
When I think about Schwab U.S. Broad Market ETF (SCHB), I think of a benchmark in the US large-cap market. And when I think about the US large-cap market, today I think … “What a mess!”

Yes, because there are 3 big reasons to be bullish, 2 fundamental and 1 technical. But also 2 great reasons to stay away from it.

In my opinion, if it weren’t for the last of these, I would have given a BUY rating. For now I remain on HOLD. Before explaining why …

What is SCHB

It is a passive ETF designed as “core” exposure to the US equity market belonging to the Large Blend, broad category. The stated benchmark is the Dow Jones U.S. Broad Stock Market Index, which includes about 2,500 listed U.S. companies. The stated objective is to replicate it as closely as possible. Structural implication? The strategy aims for market beta, not alpha;

What has made it widely used is its cost: 0.03%, an extremely competitive cost and practically in line with the main broad market/large-cap peers, fully offset by a 30-day SEC Yield of 1.15%. Not surprisingly, today it has an AUM above $39 billion.

Composition

The index it tracks uses a float-adjusted market capitalization weighted method, with sampling techniques, which produces a “winner-takes-all” type dynamic, and like other indices, the fund tends to reflect the leadership of mega caps and to participate mainly in rallies driven by Big Tech and growth. Here the top ten account for over 30%, and strong exposures can be seen to stocks such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Apple Inc. (AAPL), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (and the other Mag 7).

Peers

In this sense, the comparison with ETFs that have the S&P 500 as a benchmark, such as the famous and widely used SPY, can be interesting. This is a tabular reworking of SA data that can help.

In essence, there are no major differences except that SPY is slightly more tech-heavy. This also means fairly similar valuations; trying to weight by % of sector allocation, this is, in my opinion, a good comparison in valuation terms:

Which brings the final analysis directly back to performance, which naturally slightly favors SPY since 2022 due to the boom in the tech segment.

Despite this, I prefer SCHB for a very simple reason: a lower expense ratio (SPY has an expense ratio of 0.09%), compared to SCHB’s 2397 holdings versus SPY’s 504.

Does It Make Sense Today?

Considering SCHB as a potential core solution to cover the US component of a portfolio, the question I ask myself today is, given these valuations, does it make sense to take a position today from a forward-looking perspective? My opinion: “It depends.”

  1. Good forward valuations: The forward valuations of the most sensitive component of SCHB (info tech) have now returned close to the average. In theory, with valuations at 20.3x, we are at almost the 50th percentile of the 10-year distribution and above the 74th of the 30-year one.
  2. Rates already high: Powell over the last three years has not made the mistake of cutting interest rates below the neutral level, and the fed funds are already at 3.5-3.75%. According to the CME FedWatch tool, the probabilities of a hike today are at 2.6% (despite inflation expectations having risen). In fact, the 1Y break-even inflation has exceeded 4.5%, but the 5- and 10-year ones remain within the pre-energy shock threshold of March. If energy inflation were actually only temporarily contagious, and the conflict were to end in the short term as suggested by the (provocative) communications of the White House, this “flash war” would have generated a discount relative to expected valuations that is attractive.
  3. Technical evidence: I am not a fan of using technical analysis unless the fundamental analysis has already given me elements to reason on. And I do not use many indicators, only 1: RSI on a weekly timeframe set between 30 and 70 points. Nothing exceptional. But extraordinary, in my opinion, for those like me who have long horizons. And the reasoning is simple. Since 2012, the RSI lows (30 points) have been tactical zones for those working with DCA.

Risk

So far, it seems like a rare opportunity. But if we introduce these two risks, we immediately realize that the “discount” is not exactly free.

  • CapEx does not lead to a sufficient increase in margins to justify these earnings expectations. If EPS fell, then forward valuations were not convenient; in fact, it was a trap. And a clue in this sense is seen when, instead of forward P/E, we use forward P/FCF as a metric. The “discount” (if we can call it that) contract (in fact, disappears).

  • The conflict in Iran, even without worsening costs for companies (higher interest rates), can impact revenues (contraction in consumption), still reducing EPS. This would make valuations unsustainable.

My Opinion

So it is true that forward valuations have returned to the average, but are the historical valuations (10 and 30 years) of the tech segment consistent with the characteristics of today’s sector? Because if CAPEX increases, tech companies become capital-intensive assets, like industrials. And those trade at very different forward valuations, lower.

On this point, it should be considered that big tech, from one year to another, has increased CapEx by +70%. And it is the same reason why the expected EPS of the tech sector is so high (above 30% for 2026). Therefore, looking at P/FCF today, in my opinion, is wrong if one wants to have a forward-looking view over the next 5/10 years.

What really worries the market, in my opinion, is the second point: the idea that inflation could contract consumption, not only in the tech segment, although it is mainly the one driving the declines of recent days. But in this sense, two of the 3 reasons listed, in my opinion, precisely mitigate this risk.

For this reason, I think the right rating is HOLD: valuations are attractive, even net of P/FCF (in my view temporarily high), but the economic impact will mainly depend on the evolution of the energy context, therefore indirectly on geopolitical reasons (outside the forward control of the investor).

Conclusion

To conclude, valuations compress if we look at forward valuations. I do not think that the lack of FCF can be a problem. The fact that the economic impact is difficult to estimate makes it hard to say whether these valuations are realistically at a discount. But in relative terms, today they are, also from a technical point of view. For this reason, I think a SELL rating is not justified at all.
